product_summary.one_sentence_summary: Sector-specific French training for Luxembourg workplaces that need practical French for finance, healthcare, legal/admin and IT contexts.
product_summary.target_user: Cross-border employees and local teams in Luxembourg who need French for work; buying motion likely sits with HR/L&D or business leaders.
product_summary.core_pain: General French courses do not map well enough to sector vocabulary, workplace documents and client or compliance communication.
product_summary.claimed_outcome: Better workplace French in sector contexts and easier use of company training co-funding.

primary_buyer_type.reason: The card is explicitly B2B and B2C, so the buying motion is shared between individual learners and employer-side buyers.

secondary_buyer_types.reason: Employer-sponsored language upskilling usually sits with HR/L&D or training teams.
The course is framed as a workplace capability product, so companies are likely to sponsor it.
The card also prices the offer per person, so self-paying learners remain a real channel.

budget_owner.reason: Budget could come from an individual learner or from an employer training budget depending on the channel.

buyer_influencers.reason: HR/L&D influences training selection and co-funding administration.
Managers care whether language training actually improves day-to-day work output.
Training budget and co-funding logic make finance a likely influencer in larger firms.
In SMEs, founder or CEO approval is often needed for training spend.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: INFPC company training co-funding exists at 15% of annual training investment, with a 35% salary-cost increase for some employee categories.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: Luxembourg public sources confirm the general company-training co-funding scheme and the 15% / 35% structure for eligible cases.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: There are 228K cross-border workers in the Luxembourg market.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: STATEC reports about 229.1k non-resident cross-border wage-earners in 2024, so the card's figure is directionally close but not exact and the scope is slightly different.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: About 47% of the workforce is cross-border.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: The official 2024 figures imply a cross-border share in the mid-40% range, so the card's number is close but not exact.

externally_checkable_claims.claim_text: Nobody does sector-specialized French plus INFPC co-funding positioning.
externally_checkable_claims.notes: Public substitutes already exist: Prolingua offers professional, business and specialist language training including French, and INLL offers French courses for professional purposes. Company training co-funding also exists independently of this product, so the exclusivity claim is not defensible.

internal_claims_unverified.claim_text: Sector-specific French is the right wedge because workplace French for contracts, meetings and compliance is more valuable than general French.
internal_claims_unverified.reason_unverified: This is a positioning inference from the product card, not an externally verified market fact.

internal_claims_unverified.claim_text: Teachers are ready and the LMS is ready.
internal_claims_unverified.reason_unverified: Readiness is an internal operational claim and was not independently verified.

internal_claims_unverified.claim_text: Pilot on existing B2B clients is a low-risk next step.
internal_claims_unverified.reason_unverified: This is a planning judgment rather than an externally checkable fact.

funding_bodies.name: INFPC
funding_bodies.relevance: The public co-funding scheme is the main funding route referenced by the product card.

regulatory_requirements.requirement: Private-sector companies legally established in Luxembourg and carrying out their main business there can apply for co-funding for employee training, subject to eligibility conditions.

subsidies_or_funding.name: Company training co-funding
subsidies_or_funding.eligibility_notes: General employer training support exists. The card's sector-specific French positioning is not itself a special funding category, but the training can plausibly sit inside the normal co-funding route if the employer and employees meet the conditions.

competitors_or_substitutes.name: Prolingua
competitors_or_substitutes.notes: Offers French, business language, specialist language and tailor-made professional courses in Luxembourg.

competitors_or_substitutes.name: INLL French intensive courses for professional purposes
competitors_or_substitutes.notes: Public provider offering intensive French courses for professional purposes and CEFR-based language training.

competitors_or_substitutes.name: Allingua corporate language training
competitors_or_substitutes.notes: Corporate language training in Luxembourg with sector adaptation, CEFR-aligned testing and HR reporting.

legal_claim_severity.reason: The key externally relevant claim is co-funding / training-aid positioning, not a regulated or accredited pathway.

authority_needed_for_delivery.reason: General delivery is straightforward, but sector-specific content benefits from external SME review for accuracy.

authority_needed_for_claims.reason: Co-funding and sector-specific claims should be checked by an external reviewer, but no formal licence is required.

proof_stack.reason: The main commercial proof is whether employers and learners choose this over generic French training.
A paid pilot is the fastest way to validate willingness to buy the sector-specific framing.
The product should show measurable French improvement in workplace contexts.

proof_burden.reason: This is a standard B2B/B2C training product, so commercial uptake and learning progress matter more than formal recognition.

dependency_types.reason: The sector vocabulary and workplace examples are Luxembourg-specific.
Finance, healthcare and legal-administrative content needs sector review to avoid shallow examples.
The commercial story relies partly on company co-funding logic.

scope_variance_risk.reason: The current scope is narrowly Luxembourg-based and language-specific.

expansion_variance_risk.reason: Expanding into other countries or more regulated professions would change vocabulary, buyer expectations and funding logic.

subsidy_exists.reason: Luxembourg has a public co-funding scheme for company training.

subsidy_eligibility_status.reason: The product is normal employer training rather than a compulsory regulated-profession course, so it plausibly fits the general co-funding route.

subsidy_conflict_flag.reason: The public co-funding scheme exists and no direct conflict with the product category was found, though product-level eligibility still needs review.

market_regulatory_context.reason: The product is a workplace language training offer, not a regulated-practice or licensing product.

claim_language_risk.reason: The exclusivity language around sector-specialized French is stronger than the evidence supports.

authority_dependency_risk.reason: Sector-specific credibility depends on subject-matter review even though no formal approval is needed.

competitive_gap_risk.reason: Public substitutes already cover professional, specialist and tailor-made language training in Luxembourg.

numeric_claim_risk.reason: The cross-border and workforce-share numbers are directionally supported but not exact.

subsidy_conflict_risk.reason: The general co-funding route is real and the product does not obviously conflict with it.

positioning_risk.reason: The product sits close to generic professional French unless the sector packaging is genuinely deep.

claim_conflicts.claim_text: Nobody does sector-specialized French plus INFPC co-funding positioning.
claim_conflicts.what_is_wrong: There are already professional, business and specialist French offers in Luxembourg, and the co-funding mechanism exists independently of this product. The market is not empty and the exclusivity claim is too strong.
The official figures are close but not exact, and the precise denominator differs depending on whether the card is referring to wage-earners, all employment, or another workforce measure.

recommended_manual_review.reason: The core product is only defensible if the sector-specific curriculum is genuinely differentiated from standard professional French.
Keep the INFPC claim generic until the eligibility path is checked against the exact employer and employee profile.

sources.title: Learn to speak French with our language courses
Our Language Training Solutions in Luxembourg
